title: RFE: Podman pod Split/Podman pod attach/Podman pod detach
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

Currently I have two ideas:

`podman pod detach $CID` and `podman pod attach $CID` in terms of attaching and detaching a container to/from a pod. I like the idea of pods having their own commands that don't utilize container commands explicitly. This is the simpler of the two ideas and makes sense in that a user might want to add or remove an existing container from a pod but not have to create a new one or entirely remove it from the host.

Below is the original idea to split the pod into its containers which isn't straightforward.

*Purpose* 
Retrieve a pod and all of its containers (not infra). Split the pod into all of its containers, recreating whatever namespaces, devices, or volumes necessary. This could be an interesting application of pods in which the user wants to remove all containers or possibly a single container (by ID/Name) from a pod

*Issues*
Recreating or retrieving certain information will be tricky. This is why we should begin work on this now before all pod creation options are exposed. I believe with the current implementation, we could easily extract key information for the “new” containers such as NS, Devices, Volumes. 

Might need to make modifications of the current process of updating the pod state, since we are going to need to alter certain configured entities. This might also involve adding new state values so we can check things easily.

Cgroup recreation alongside NS recreation will be the trickiest. Best solution is to make a new private NS and new Cgroup for the container (if possible, feedback on this part is probably needed)

*Roadmap*
Begin with two or three options notably `--id` and `--containers` (optional) to specify certain containers we want to split from the pod

Over time add more complicated flags once the feature actually works, other flags could includes things you want to omit from the split containers like network settings or resource limits

comments.body:
Thanks for the nice write-up, @cdoern.  I sympathize in spirit but I have reservations due to the entailed complexity.  It brings up the "pet vs cattle" idea behind containers.

Does checkpoint & restore work in that case?

I think this boils down to `podman container clone` which @mheon has been working on.

Also do we need such a feature, why would a user want to split the pod into individual containers? Is there any user demand for it?

Difficult - it violates our immutable container promises, which makes it
quite complicated. Best  suggestion I have is to do something similar to
the first version of rename - remove the container (but not from storage)
and then add it again to the DB after changes.

On Mon, Sep 27, 2021 at 10:35 Charlie Doern ***@***.***>
wrote:

> @vrothberg <https://github.com/vrothberg> @Luap99
> <https://github.com/Luap99> thanks for the feedback. I guess I just see a
> sort of gap in pod commands and was looking to fill it. Maybe there is a
> simpler version of what I am thinking where we can split a single container
> out of a pod but keep it in existence.
>
> I do see the similarity to podman container clone but I feel as though it
> would make sense to maybe make something like podman pod detach $CID and podman
> pod attach $CID in terms of attaching and detaching a container to/from a
> pod. I like the idea of pods having their own commands that don't utilize
> container commands explicitly.
>
> @rhatdan <https://github.com/rhatdan> @mheon <https://github.com/mheon>
> thoughts on a surefire way to add/remove containers from a pod once already
> created?
>
> —

Ok @mheon, I am going to do some messing around with this to see if it is possible and/or worthwhile.
I still don't see the use case.  Please explain why anyone would ever do this?

> I still don't see the use case.  Please explain why anyone would ever do this?

The main use case I see would be as follows:

You create a pod with the distinct purpose of limiting CPU usage of the containers within it. Let's call it `cpuPod`. If you have a container running that for some reason you want to keep the container config, logs etc, and you want to modify the CPU usage you can execute `podman pod attach cpuPod $CID` causing the container to be maintained but the state updated with the new cgroup conf.

This idea can be applied to anything like volume, devices etc. 

The idea of giving a container access to new limits or data without removing it and making a new one is the main point of this. It can even be used to temporarily add and remove containers from a pod just to gain the information and configuration of the other containers.

Not sure if that makes sense, if not I'll stop investigating but if it could be useful I'd happily take a shot at it.
My gut feeling is that it's a comparatively expensive feature (not yet sure about all implications) that will be used extremely rarely.

@vrothberg do you think `pod attach` would be worthwhile? I feel like this could be useful casually when one forgets to do `--pod` and gaining the pods volumes or devices could be interesting. If not I'll just stick to my pod create options :)
I think the imbalance of effort and frequency of use is still high.  I see containers as something (mostly) immutable; persistent state should reside outside of the container (e.g., on a volume).

I think this should be done in a new container.  Containers are easy to create and destroy.  So I agree this is way to complex for almost anyone to ever need or want.  
We can discuss it futher the next time you attend a meeting, but for now I am closing this.
